answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: The IAG New Zealand entity level climate scenarios – Orderly, Disorderly and Hot House World – are outlined in Figure 3... Orderly: Net Zero 2050 – An ambitious and coordinated transition aligned with a 1.5°C warming trajectory; Disorderly: Delayed Transition – Delayed action, followed by sudden and uncoordinated transformation, landing at <2.0°C; Hot House World: Current Policies – Continuation of current policy settings, leading to uncontrolled warming of 3°C+
answers.notes: These are IAG New Zealand's entity-level scenarios, adopted from the ICNZ (Insurance Council of New Zealand) scenarios with adjusted time horizons.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: 1.5°C (Orderly/Net Zero 2050), below 2.0°C (Disorderly/Delayed Transition), and 3°C+ (Hot House World/Current Policies). RCP ranges are also given: RCP 2.6 (0.3°C to 1.8°C), RCP 4.5 (0.7°C to 3.3°C), RCP 6.0 (1.2°C to 4.3°C).
answers.evidence: Orderly: Net Zero 2050 (1.5°C warming trajectory) – "Global warming was successfully limited to 1.5°C." Disorderly: Delayed Transition (<2.0°C) – "Global warming was limited to below 2°C by 2050." Hot House World: Current Policies (3+°C) – "resulting in a failure to limit total global warming to 2°C." RCP: RCP 2.6 (0.3°C to 1.8°C); RCP: RCP 4.5 (0.7°C to 3.3°C); RCP: RCP 6.0 (1.2°C to 4.3°C)
answers.notes: Temperature outcomes are expressed as scenario targets/trajectories rather than explicit 2100 projections, though RCP ranges are provided. The Disorderly scenario references "below 2°C by 2050" rather than 2100.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: The Insurance Council of New Zealand (ICNZ) is the primary scenario provider. The Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) approach was predominantly used to support the creation of the ICNZ scenarios. The Climate Change Commission (CCC) of New Zealand is also referenced as a pathway source.
answers.evidence: IAG New Zealand was involved in creating the Insurance Council of New Zealand | Te Kāhui Inihua o Aotearoa insurance sector climate scenarios (ICNZ scenarios). Those scenarios have been used as a basis for the development of our own entity-level scenarios... The Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) approach was predominantly used to support the creation of the ICNZ scenarios and the Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSP) scenarios were used as an input, noting that other data sources were also considered as part of these processes where appropriate.
answers.notes: ICNZ is the direct scenario provider; NGFS is the underlying framework used to construct the ICNZ scenarios. IAG New Zealand also participated in creating the ICNZ scenarios.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: Pathways: Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS), Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSP), Adjusted Shared Policy Assumptions New Zealand (SPANZ), Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) and Climate Change Commission (CCC). [For Orderly:] SSP: SSP1: Sustainability; RCP: RCP 2.6 (0.3°C to 1.8°C). [For Disorderly:] SSP: SSP2: Middle of the Road; RCP: RCP 4.5 (0.7°C to 3.3°C). [For Hot House World:] SSP: SSP5: Fossil-Fuelled Development; RCP: RCP 6.0 (1.2°C to 4.3°C).
answers.notes: The document explicitly lists both SSP and RCP identifiers for each scenario. SSPs are from CMIP6 and RCPs are from CMIP5 — these are from different scenario generations and are not internally consistent families. The document pairs them together within each scenario.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: IAG New Zealand was involved in creating the Insurance Council of New Zealand | Te Kāhui Inihua o Aotearoa insurance sector climate scenarios (ICNZ scenarios). Those scenarios have been used as a basis for the development of our own entity-level scenarios. During the development of our own scenarios, we reviewed and adjusted the ICNZ scenario short-term and medium-term time horizons... The short-term horizon of 2022-2025 was adjusted to 2024-2027 to reflect our rolling three-year strategic planning and budget cycle outlook... The medium-term horizon of 2026-2035 was adjusted to 2028-2035 to reflect the above change.
answers.notes: The ICNZ scenarios were themselves adapted for the New Zealand insurance sector context (using SPANZ — Adjusted Shared Policy Assumptions New Zealand — and CCC pathways). IAG New Zealand then further customised these by adjusting time horizons to align with their own strategic planning cycle. The physical risk models also had "overlays applied to reflect the projected physical risk changes across a range of natural peril types."

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: IAG New Zealand was involved in creating the Insurance Council of New Zealand | Te Kāhui Inihua o Aotearoa insurance sector climate scenarios (ICNZ scenarios). Those scenarios have been used as a basis for the development of our own entity-level scenarios.
answers.notes: The document states IAG New Zealand was "involved in creating" the ICNZ scenarios, which implies collaborative/participatory development with the Insurance Council of New Zealand (an industry body). However, the exact nature of the participation (e.g., whether it was a formal co-design process with broader stakeholders) is not explicitly described. No keywords like "participatory", "co-creat", "deliberat", or "co-design" are used directly.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: Yes — the "Hot House World: Current Policies" scenario functions as a baseline/reference scenario, representing continuation of current policy settings. The CCC (Climate Change Commission) "Current policy reference" pathway is also explicitly named as the corresponding CCC pathway for this scenario.
answers.evidence: Hot House World: Current Policies – Continuation of current policy settings, leading to uncontrolled warming of 3°C+... CCC: Current policy reference... Economic growth is high, combined with material-intensive production and consumption patterns and a strong reliance on abundant fossil fuel resources.
answers.notes: The document does not explicitly label any scenario as a "baseline" or "reference" scenario against which others are compared. However, the "Hot House World: Current Policies" scenario and its associated CCC "Current policy reference" pathway serve this function by representing a continuation of current policies without additional climate action.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: An ARIMAX regression model was applied to historical data series and identified key explanatory variables (real consumption growth per working age person) and change in the unemployment rate level. Existing Deloitte macroeconomic forecasts for those variables were then used to derive vehicles per working age population forecast baseline, and then adjusted to vehicles per capita.
answers.notes: The ARIMAX model is used for the vehicles-per-capita metric, not for the core climate scenario analysis. No named climate models (e.g., GCMs, IAMs) are explicitly identified for the scenario analysis itself. The document references "physical risk models underpinning our insurance and reinsurance risk assessments" and a "vendor model for flood hazard incidence" but these are not named.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Our scenario analysis also leveraged the physical risk models underpinning our insurance and reinsurance risk assessments, with overlays applied to reflect the projected physical risk changes across a range of natural peril types. This analysis helps us investigate the sensitivity of our insurance portfolios to climate-related risk... During FY24 IAG New Zealand customers made claims arising from 199 major weather-related events across the country... Weather-related catastrophe coded events including storms, floods, cyclones, wind and wildfires where the total cost of related claims for an event is greater than $1M.
answers.notes: The scenario analysis explicitly incorporates acute physical risk (natural perils including storms, floods, cyclones, wind, wildfires) through physical risk models with overlays. Flood risk modelling is also described in detail in Appendix B. The document also discusses the sensitivity of insurance portfolios to these risks.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: Pathways: Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS), Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSP), Adjusted Shared Policy Assumptions New Zealand (SPANZ), Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) and Climate Change Commission (CCC). Analysis conducted during the development of the ICNZ scenarios determined that energy pathways, carbon sequestration from afforestation; and negative emissions technology do not have a material impact on the insurance sector... The potential for a step-change in coastal hazards (driven by sea level rise approaching the threshold levels around which our coastal communities have been designed)... Changes to the impacts from ex-tropical cyclones... Changes to the physical size, longevity and intensity of severe convective storms... Emergence of wildfire risk... Potential non-linear responses to compounding wind and rain damage... Increased liquefaction risk driven by rising groundwater levels impacted by sea level rise in coastal areas... Increased tsunami risk driven by rising sea levels... Economic growth is high, combined with material-intensive production and consumption patterns and a strong reliance on abundant fossil fuel resources.
answers.notes: Many of these variables are discussed as risk factors or scenario characteristics rather than explicit quantified scenario drivers. The document does not always clearly distinguish between scenario inputs and scenario outputs/impacts.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: The physical risk modelling approach is limited by the lack of region-specific, high-quality scientific literature addressing future-climate severe weather events (weather that happens in a wholly different state of the climate) in Aotearoa New Zealand... Our flood model relies upon council flood studies and a vendor model for flood hazard incidence when a council study is unavailable. Flood modelling is uncertain in Aotearoa New Zealand as there is regional variation in the quality and availability of elevation data and local flood studies... The Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) approach was predominantly used to support the creation of the ICNZ scenarios and the Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSP) scenarios were used as an input.
answers.location_hint: Appendix B, p.19; Appendix B, p.22; Section 2, p.7 footnote 5
answers.notes: The transition scenarios draw on global frameworks (NGFS, SSP, RCP) while the physical risk modelling is applied at a New Zealand/regional level using local flood studies and council data. The SPANZ (Adjusted Shared Policy Assumptions New Zealand) pathway is also a New Zealand-specific adaptation.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short-term: 2024–2027; Medium-term: 2028–2035; Long-term: 2036–2050. Rationale is provided for the short- and medium-term adjustments, but not for the long-term horizon.
answers.evidence: The short-term horizon of 2022-2025 was adjusted to 2024-2027 to reflect our rolling three-year strategic planning and budget cycle outlook and was made in consideration of our Enterprise Risk Profile timeframe. This time horizon also aligns with our initial capital deployment to enhance risk modelling currently being conducted by the Natural Perils Uplift Project... The medium-term horizon of 2026-2035 was adjusted to 2028-2035 to reflect the above change. We adopted the long-term horizon in the ICNZ scenarios (2036-2050).
answers.notes: The short-term horizon is justified by alignment with the rolling three-year strategic planning cycle and Enterprise Risk Profile timeframe, and the Natural Perils Uplift Project. The medium-term adjustment follows from the short-term change. The long-term horizon (2036–2050) was adopted from the ICNZ scenarios without further justification provided.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.answer: Yes — partial justification is provided. The ICNZ scenarios were chosen as the basis because IAG New Zealand was involved in creating them (as an industry-relevant set of scenarios). The NGFS approach was used as the predominant framework for the ICNZ scenarios. Time horizon adjustments are justified by alignment with internal planning cycles.
answers.evidence: IAG New Zealand was involved in creating the Insurance Council of New Zealand | Te Kāhui Inihua o Aotearoa insurance sector climate scenarios (ICNZ scenarios). Those scenarios have been used as a basis for the development of our own entity-level scenarios... The Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) approach was predominantly used to support the creation of the ICNZ scenarios and the Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSP) scenarios were used as an input, noting that other data sources were also considered as part of these processes where appropriate... The short-term horizon of 2022-2025 was adjusted to 2024-2027 to reflect our rolling three-year strategic planning and budget cycle outlook and was made in consideration of our Enterprise Risk Profile timeframe.
answers.notes: The justification is implicit rather than a formal, explicit statement of why these specific scenarios were chosen over alternatives. The document explains the provenance of the scenarios (ICNZ, NGFS) and the rationale for time horizon adjustments, but does not provide a formal comparative justification for why these scenarios were selected over other available frameworks.
